learning_outcomes.description: Describe the importance of soils for regulating plant growth, food production, and climate change
Evaluate how soils interact with the broader environment, including their influence on a range of environmental processes
Explain the role of agriculture production systems in the sustainable management of soils
Use soil water relationships to examine plant water use and optimise irrigation systems
Experimentally investigate approaches for solving a range of soil science problems, including for management of soil degradation

assessments.task_description:
Practical assessment is divided into 4 x practical hands-on sessions followed by the preparation of a written practical report.

Each report will be based on specific practical classes. Practical Classes are scheduled every 2-3 weeks. The practicals run for approximately 3 hours and are a combination of laboratory-based and field-based activities. To obtain a mark for the practical classes students must participate and complete the associated exercises (submitting the practical report via Blackboard - Turnitin). If you do not participate in a practical class, or do not provide evidence of your attendance (i.e. signing attendance sheet), your report will not be marked/graded. Refer to late submission notes for more information.

Details about the Practical will be provided within a Practical Manual, which will be made available on Blackboard site before the scheduled practical, with additional specifics about report preparation and report submission.

  • Report 1: Field assessment of soil properties and constraints.

Students will be able to describe a soil profile using field methods to assess the suitability, limitations of the soil and potential of the soil for plant growth and potential land use. This report will assess learning objectives 1, 2 & 5.

During this field experience, students undertake hands-on soil profile description following nationally recognised protocols, including those outlined in the Australian Soil and Land Survey Field Handbook and the Australian Soil Classification. Students are trained to observe, measure, interpret, and document soil physical and chemical properties in situ, mirroring professional practice in soil survey, land capability assessment, agricultural consulting, and environmental assessment.

Specifically, students:

  • Identify and delineate soil horizons in intact profiles;
  • Apply field techniques to determine soil texture, structure, aggregate stability, colour (Munsell system), soil strength (penetrometer), pH, and carbonate presence;
  • Assess soil constraints (e.g. compaction, acidity, texture contrast, drainage limitations);
  • Classify soils using the Australian Soil Classification system and interpret diagnostic features;
  • Link observed soil properties to land use suitability, management constraints, and environmental risk.

Students record their observations using a professional soil profile description card adapted from industry practice, and subsequently prepare an assessed technical report that requires interpretation of field data, justification of soil classification, identification of key constraints, and recommendations for appropriate land use and management options.

  • Report 2: Effects of organic matter on soil properties.

Students will investigate the effect of various organic matter sources on soil properties including respiration and bulk density. This report will assess learning objectives 2, 3 & 5.

In this practical, students examine how organic matter additions influence physical, chemical and biological soil functions. The activity introduces students to the role of soil organic matter in nutrient cycling, cation exchange capacity, water-holding capacity, aggregate formation, microbial activity and carbon cycling. Students compare different organic amendments or fertiliser/amendment treatments and assess their effects on soil respiration by measuring CO₂ accumulation during a short incubation period. The practical further develops students’ understanding of soil physical properties through measurements or interpretation of bulk density and porosity, helping them connect organic matter management to soil structure, aeration, water storage and root growth potential.

  • Report 3: Irrigation system design and evaluation.

Students will set up and compare different types of drip irrigation systems to evaluate their advantages and limitations. This report will assess learning objectives 4 & 5.

In this practical, students investigate the principles of agricultural irrigation, with a focus on drip irrigation efficiency and uniformity. Students compare different drip line designs and operating pressures to evaluate how irrigation system specifications influence water discharge along the length of the system. Students measure water discharge from emitters, assess pressure changes along the drip line, and use the collected data to evaluate irrigation uniformity, total water application and potential limitations in system design. The activity helps students understand how irrigation design affects water distribution, crop water supply, water-use efficiency and the risk of under- or over-irrigation.

  • Report 4: Fertilizer (organic vs inorganic) effects on plant growth.

Students will set up a glasshouse trial comparing the effect of mineral and organic fertilisers on a test crop growth. This report will assess learning objectives 2, 3 & 5.

In this practical, students investigate how different fertiliser sources influence plant growth under controlled glasshouse conditions. The activity compares organic and mineral fertilisers with similar nutrient ratios, allowing students to consider not only nutrient supply but also differences in nutrient availability, mineralisation, organic matter inputs and soil biological activity. Students establish a pot trial using different soils and fertiliser treatments, with replication and a control treatment. Plant growth responses are assessed through measurements such as plant height, number of leaves, biomass production and visual observations of plant condition.

Absence from a Practical Session

The Practical sessions are timetabled activities, and if students miss a practical and require access to online material, they must follow the process outlined below. Documentation that shows they were unable to attend the practical on the relevant day will be needed (note that documentation for ongoing conditions, such as Student Access Plans, are not acceptable evidence for missed Practical sessions).

If you are unable to complete your timetabled practical session because of circumstances beyond your control (e.g., illness) you must submit a practical absence request immediately accompanied by acceptable supporting documentation.

A list of acceptable reasons and required supporting documentation is found at https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/exams-and-assessment/applying-assessment-extension?p=1#1

Your request must be submitted within 24 hours of the missed practical. The submission link can be found in the Learning Resources (Blackboard Original) or Course Resources (Blackboard Ultra) folder in your course Blackboard site.  

Requests without the correct supporting documentation may not be approved. If the reason for your absence is not approved, you will be considered absent from that practical session.

It is your responsibility to submit an application and provide correct documentation. If you do not attend the prac and do not have approval, you are unable to obtain marks for the corresponding portions of the prac report.

  • If there is an associated assessment item, you must still submit the relevant assessment item by the due date. If the reason for your absence impacts your ability to meet the due date, you must apply for an extension of assessment due date.
  • This course is unable to provide an alternative session to attend.
  • If you require access to restricted online resources to complete your assessment, you must contact the course coordinator to get access to the online resources, following confirmation of your approved Practical Absence.

If you require further assistance, please do not hesitate to contact AGFS student administration - agriculture@enquire.uq.edu.au. 

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. 

A failure to reference and acknowledged generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

The use of AI or MT must be referenced and acknowledged appropriately. Please see assessment guidelines in blackboard course site for specific instructions. Students may also refer to Library guides found under Additional assessment information > Acknowledging and referencing AI use.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
In-person exam covering all course content.

The exam will be an on-campus exam. Have your UQ student ID card available for all your exams.  

Students enrolled in In Person delivery must attend the exam in-person.

learning_activities.topic:
Module 1: The functions of soil properties and its components

This module introduces students to the fundamental role of soils in supporting agricultural production, environmental quality and land management. Students examine global land use and the key functions of soil, before exploring the major soil components, including mineral particles, organic matter, water, air and biological organisms. The module develops students’ understanding of how soil properties influence plant growth, nutrient cycling, water movement and environmental risk.

Students investigate the central role of organic matter in soil structure, fertility, biological activity and carbon dynamics. They also examine soil fertility principles, including nutrient budgets and the behaviour of key nutrients such as nitrogen and phosphorus, as well as alternative nutrient inputs. The module concludes by considering major soil degradation processes, including salinity, sodicity, acidity and alkalinity, and how these constraints affect soil function and sustainable land use.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02

The module develops students’ understanding of how soil properties influence plant growth, nutrient cycling, water movement and environmental risk. Students investigate the central role of organic matter in soil structure, fertility, biological activity and carbon dynamics. They also examine soil fertility principles, including nutrient budgets and the behaviour of key nutrients such as nitrogen and phosphorus, as well as alternative nutrient inputs. The module concludes by considering major soil degradation processes, including salinity, sodicity, acidity and alkalinity, and how these constraints affect soil function and sustainable land use.

learning_activities.topic:
Module 2: Sustainable agricultural management practices

This module focuses on the practical management of soils for sustainable agricultural production. Students examine the importance of soil structure, soil water management and irrigation in maintaining productive and resilient farming systems. They explore how different irrigation systems operate, how water use can be improved, and how soil physical properties influence infiltration, drainage, water storage and crop access to water.

The module also considers broader soil management practices, including erosion control, maintenance of soil biodiversity and ecology, use of organic amendments, and strategies for managing soil contamination. Students are introduced to emerging and applied approaches such as agrophysics and reflect on the challenges of sustainable agriculture in Australia. Overall, the module links soil science principles to practical decision-making in agricultural, environmental and land management contexts.

Learning outcomes: L03, L04
